lessons.text: Notice fear grip your body before a hard task.
lessons.rationale: Captures Arjuna's visceral panic—failing limbs, parched mouth, trembling, the bow slipping—as the felt onset of fear before an overwhelming task, not an abstract mood.
lessons.old_text: Notice when dread seizes your body before a hard duty.

verses.text: Arjuna said, "O Krishna, seeing my kinsmen arrayed here, eager to fight,
My limbs fail, my mouth is parched, my body quivers, and my hair stands on end.
The Gandiva slips from my hand, and my skin burns all over; I am unable to stand, and my mind is reeling, as it were.

lessons.text: Some wins bring only guilt.
lessons.rationale: Anchors to Arjuna's insight that slaying his kin yields no good and only sin—distinct from the fear lesson because it judges the outcome of the act, not the emotion before it.
lessons.old_text: Some victories bring only guilt, never happiness.

verses.text: And I see ill omens, O Kesava. I do not see any good in slaying my kinsmen in battle.
By killing these sons of Dhritarashtra, what pleasure could be ours, O Janardana? Only sin would accrue to us from killing these felons.
Therefore, we should not kill the sons of Dhritarashtra, our relatives; for how can we be happy by killing our own kin, O Madhava (Krishna)?

lessons.text: No prize is worth ruining people you love.
lessons.rationale: Specific to Arjuna's rejection of kingdom and pleasures once he sees that the very people he'd win them for stand before him as the cost—about the worthlessness of the reward itself.
lessons.old_text: Refuse a prize bought with the ruin of those you love.

verses.text:
I desire not victory, O Krishna, nor kingdom, nor pleasures.
What use is dominion to us, O Krishna, or pleasures or even life?
Those for whose sake we desire kingdom, enjoyments, and pleasures stand here in battle, having renounced life and wealth.
Teachers, fathers, sons, and grandfathers, maternal uncles, fathers-in-law, grandsons, brothers-in-law, and other relatives—
These I do not wish to kill, O Krishna, even though they kill me, for the sake of dominion over the three worlds; leave alone killing them for the sake of the earth."

lessons.text: Others' greed is no excuse for your own.
lessons.rationale: Draws on Arjuna contrasting the enemy's greed-blinded conscience with his own clear sight, and his admission of being swept toward sin by greed—about moral responsibility despite what others excuse.
lessons.old_text: When greed blinds others to wrong, still choose to turn away.

verses.text: Though they, with intelligence overpowered by greed, see no evil in the destruction of families and no sin in hostility to friends,
Why should we not, who clearly see the evil in the destruction of families, learn to turn away from this sin, O Janardana (Krishna)?
Alas! We are involved in a great sin, for we are prepared to kill our kinsmen, out of greed for the pleasures of a kingdom.

lessons.text: One reckless act can wound generations.
lessons.rationale: Captures Arjuna's argument that destroying a family collapses its traditions, order, and lineage far beyond the battlefield—distinct as consequence spreading outward through time and society.
lessons.old_text: Weigh how one destructive act can unravel generations.

verses.text: In the destruction of a family, the immemorial religious rites of that family perish; on the destruction of spirituality, impiety indeed, overwhelms the whole family.
O Krishna, by the prevalence of impiety, the women of the family become corrupt; and, when women are corrupted, O Varshenya (descendant of Vrishni), intermingling of castes arises.
Confusion of castes leads to hell for the slayers of the family, for their forebears fall, deprived of the offerings of rice-balls and libations of water.
By these evil deeds of the destroyers of the family, which cause confusion of castes, the eternal religious rites of the caste and the family are destroyed.
We have heard, O Janardana, that those men in whose families the religious practices have been destroyed are inevitably destined to dwell in hell for an unknown period.

lessons.text: Don't let despair talk you into giving up.
lessons.rationale: Isolates the moment Arjuna would rather be killed unresisting than fight—the paralysis where grief distorts judgment into passive collapse, distinct from his earlier reasoned objections.
lessons.old_text: Beware letting despair make surrender feel preferable to acting.

verses.text: If the sons of Dhritarashtra, with weapons in hand, should slay me in battle, unresisting and unarmed, that would be better for me.

lessons.text: Drop the self-pity and stand up.
lessons.rationale: Krishna's opening rebuke to shake off paralyzing despondency and stand up.
lessons.old_text: Cast off weakness of heart and rise to act.

verses.text: The Blessed Lord said, "From whence has this perilous strait come upon you, this dejection which is unworthy of you, disgraceful, and which will close the gates of heaven upon you, O Arjuna?"
Do not yield to impotence, O Arjuna, son of Pritha. It does not befit you. Cast off this mean weakness of the heart! Stand up, O conqueror of foes!

lessons.text: When you're lost, ask someone wiser.
lessons.rationale: Captures Arjuna surrendering as a disciple asking for instruction, distinct from the despair verses around it.
lessons.old_text: When your duty confuses you, seek a wise teacher.

verses.text: My heart is overpowered by the taint of pity; my mind is confused as to my duty. I ask Thee: Tell me decisively what is good for me. I am Thy disciple; instruct me, who has taken refuge in Thee.

lessons.text: Don't let grief run your life.
lessons.rationale: The opening thesis of the whole discourse: grief itself is the error.
lessons.old_text: The wise grieve for neither the living nor the dead.

verses.text: The Blessed Lord said, "You have grieved for those who should not be grieved for; yet, you speak words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead."

lessons.text: You remain yourself through every stage of life.
lessons.rationale: Anchors to the specific image of passing through life's stages into another body.
lessons.old_text: The self endures unchanged through youth, age, and death.

verses.text: Nor, at any time, was I not, nor thou, nor these rulers of men; nor, verily, shall we ever cease to be hereafter.
Just as the embodied soul passes through childhood, youth, and old age in this body, so too does it pass into another body; the steadfast one does not grieve over this.

lessons.text: Pleasure and pain both pass; ride them out.
lessons.rationale: Sense-contact brings impermanent heat and cold; the one who endures them is fit for immortality.
lessons.old_text: Bear pleasure and pain evenly; both come and go.

verses.text: The contact of the senses with the objects, O son of Kunti, which causes heat and cold, pleasure and pain, has a beginning and an end; they are impermanent; endure them bravely, O Arjuna.
That firm man, whom surely these afflictions do not, O chief among men, to whom pleasure and pain are the same, is fit for attaining immortality.

lessons.text: Grief can't undo what was always coming.
lessons.rationale: The alternative argument: even granting mortality, death is certain, so grief is pointless.
lessons.old_text: Even were death final, grief cannot undo the inevitable.

verses.text: But even if thou thinkest of It as constantly being born and constantly dying, even then, O mighty-armed one, thou shouldst not grieve.
For the born, death is certain, and for the dead, birth is certain; therefore, you should not grieve over the inevitable.
Beings are unmanifest in their beginning, manifest in their middle state, O Arjuna, and unmanifest again in their end. What is there to grieve about?

lessons.text: Don't shrink from what's yours to do.
lessons.rationale: Duty framed as obligation and opportunity, with abandonment counted as sin.
lessons.old_text: Do your rightful duty rather than shrink from it.

verses.text: Further, having regard to your duty, you should not waver, for there is nothing higher for a Kshatriya than a righteous war.
Happy are the Kshatriyas, O Arjuna! who are called to fight in such a battle that comes of its own accord as an open door to heaven.
But if you will not fight this righteous war, then having abandoned your own duty and reputation, you will incur sin.

lessons.text: Running from your work costs your good name.
lessons.rationale: The worldly reputation argument, distinct from the duty-as-sin appeal before it.
lessons.old_text: Fleeing your duty brings dishonor worse than death.

verses.text: People will also recount your everlasting dishonor; and for one who has been honored, dishonor is worse than death.
The great chariot-warriors will think that you have withdrawn from the battle out of fear, and you will be held in low esteem by those who have held you in high regard.
Your enemies, scoffing at your power, will speak many abusive words—what could be more painful than this?
Slain, you will obtain heaven; victorious, you will enjoy the earth; therefore, stand up, O son of Kunti, resolved to fight.

lessons.text: Treat win and loss the same, then act.
lessons.rationale: The pivot verse equalizing all outcomes before acting.
lessons.old_text: Meet victory and defeat, gain and loss, alike.

verses.text: Having made pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat equal, engage in battle for the sake of battle; thus, you shall not incur sin.

lessons.text: No honest effort is ever wasted.
lessons.rationale: Introduces Yoga of action: no loss, and a little of it guards from great fear.
lessons.old_text: On this path no effort is ever wasted.

verses.text: This, which has been taught to you, is wisdom concerning Sankhya. Now listen to wisdom concerning Yoga, endowed with which, O Arjuna, you shall cast off the bonds of action.
In this, there is no loss of effort, nor is there any harm produced, nor any transgression. Even a little of this knowledge protects one from great fear.

lessons.text: One clear aim beats a dozen scattered ones.
lessons.rationale: Single-pointed determination against the many-branched thoughts of the irresolute.
lessons.old_text: Hold one clear resolve; scattered aims lead nowhere.

verses.text: Here, O joy of the Kurus, there is only one single-pointed determination; many-branched and endless are the thoughts of the indecisive.

lessons.text: Chasing every reward keeps your mind restless.
lessons.rationale: Those enchanted by flowery promises of enjoyment never reach steady concentration.
lessons.old_text: Chasing pleasure and reward leaves the mind unsettled.

verses.text: The unwise, taking pleasure in the eulogizing words of the Vedas, utter flowery speech, saying, "There is nothing else," O Arjuna.

Full of desires, with heaven as their goal, (they speak words that are directed to ends) leading to new births as the result of their works, and prescribe various methods abounding in specific actions, for the attainment of pleasure and power.

For those who are attached to pleasure and power, whose minds are drawn away by such teachings, their determinate reason is not formed which is steadily bent on meditation and Samadhi (superconscious state).

lessons.text: Don't be ruled by highs, lows, or wanting more.
lessons.rationale: Transcend the three gunas, opposites, and the urge to acquire and hoard.
lessons.old_text: Rise above the pairs of opposites and possessiveness.

verses.text: The Vedas deal with the three attributes; be thou above these three attributes. O Arjuna, free yourself from the pairs of opposites and ever remain in the quality of Sattva, freed from acquisition and preservation, and be established in the Self.

lessons.text: Direct understanding outgrows secondhand answers.
lessons.rationale: The reservoir-in-a-flood image: direct knowing makes borrowed texts superfluous.
lessons.old_text: To the awakened, scripture is a well amid a flood.

verses.text: To the Brahmana who has known the Self, all the Vedas are of as much use as a reservoir of water would be in a place where there is a flood.

lessons.text: Your right is to your work, not its rewards.
lessons.rationale: The keystone teaching on action without claim to the fruit.
lessons.old_text: You have a right to your work, never its results.

verses.text: Your right is only to work, but not to its results; do not let the results of action be your motive, nor let your attachment be to inaction.

lessons.text: Stay steady through both success and failure.
lessons.rationale: Defines yoga specifically as evenness of mind while acting.
lessons.old_text: Even-mindedness in success and failure is true yoga.

verses.text: Perform action, O Arjuna, being steadfast in Yoga, abandoning attachment and balanced in success and failure; evenness of mind is called Yoga.

lessons.text: Skill is working wisely, not just working hard.
lessons.rationale: Wise action transcends good and evil, far above work driven by reward.
lessons.old_text: Ground action in wisdom; that is skill in action.

verses.text: Far lower than the Yoga of wisdom is action, O Arjuna. Seek thou refuge in wisdom; wretched are those whose motive is the fruit.
Endowed with wisdom and evenness of mind, one casts off in this life both good and evil deeds; therefore, devote yourself to Yoga; Yoga is skill in action.

lessons.text: Let go of the payoff and you're free.
lessons.rationale: Renouncing fruits specifically frees one from the bonds of rebirth.
lessons.old_text: Release the fruits of action and be freed from bondage.

verses.text: The wise, possessing knowledge, having abandoned the fruits of their actions, and being freed from the bonds of birth, go to the place which is beyond all evil.

lessons.text: See clearly and empty talk loses its pull.
lessons.rationale: Passing the mire of delusion brings indifference to what is heard and to be heard.
lessons.old_text: Cross beyond delusion and lose your taste for empty words.

verses.text: When your intellect passes beyond the mire of delusion, then you will attain indifference to what has been heard and what has yet to be heard.

lessons.text: Clarity comes when your mind stops wavering.
lessons.rationale: Steadiness of intellect in the self, not scripture, yields realization.
lessons.old_text: When your mind rests unmoved in the self, you awaken.

verses.text: When your intellect, which is perplexed by the Vedic texts you have read, stands immovable and steady in the Self, then you will attain Self-realization.

lessons.text: Real contentment comes from within, not from getting.
lessons.rationale: First mark of steady wisdom: casting off desires and being satisfied in the self.
lessons.old_text: True contentment is found within, not in fulfilled desires.

verses.text: The Blessed Lord said, "When a man completely casts off, O Arjuna, all the desires of the mind and is satisfied in the Self by the Self, then he is said to be one of steady wisdom."

lessons.text: Stay steady through sorrow, craving, and fear.
lessons.rationale: The sage undisturbed by adversity and released from attachment, fear, and anger.
lessons.old_text: Stay unshaken by sorrow and free of craving and fear.

verses.text: He whose mind is not shaken by adversity, who does not long for pleasures, and is free from attachment, fear, and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.

lessons.text: Don't cling to good news or flinch at bad.
lessons.rationale: Firm wisdom neither rejoices at fortune nor hurries from misfortune.
lessons.old_text: Neither celebrate the good nor recoil from the bad.

verses.text: He who is everywhere without attachment, upon encountering anything good or bad, neither rejoices nor hastens; his wisdom is firm.

lessons.text: Pull your attention back when the world overwhelms.
lessons.rationale: The signature tortoise image for sense-withdrawal.
lessons.old_text: Withdraw your senses as a tortoise draws in its limbs.

verses.text: When, like the tortoise which withdraws all its limbs on all sides, he withdraws his senses from the sense-objects, then his wisdom becomes steady.

lessons.text: Willpower stops the act; insight ends the craving.
lessons.rationale: Objects leave the abstinent but the underlying longing fades only on seeing the Supreme.
lessons.old_text: Abstaining curbs the act; only insight ends the craving.

verses.text: The objects of the senses turn away from the abstinent man, leaving the longing behind; but his longing also turns away upon seeing the Supreme.

lessons.text: Even the disciplined get pulled off by impulse.
lessons.rationale: A warning that turbulent senses overpower even one striving to control them.
lessons.old_text: Even the wise can be swept away by restless senses.

verses.text: The turbulent senses, O Arjuna, can violently carry away the mind of a wise person, even though they are striving to control them.

lessons.text: Master your impulses and keep your aim high.
lessons.rationale: Restraint paired with devotion to the divine steadies wisdom.
lessons.old_text: Master your senses and fix your mind on the highest.

verses.text: Having restrained them all, he should sit steadfast, intent on Me; his wisdom is steady whose senses are under control.

lessons.text: Dwelling on wanting breeds desire, then anger, then ruin.
lessons.rationale: The famous descent chain from brooding to attachment, anger, delusion, and destruction.
lessons.old_text: Dwelling on objects breeds desire, then anger, then ruin.

verses.text: When one thinks of objects, attachment to them arises; from attachment, desire is born; from desire, anger arises.
Anger leads to delusion, which causes loss of memory; this, in turn, leads to the destruction of discrimination, resulting in destruction.

lessons.text: Meet temptation calmly, neither grabbing nor fleeing.
lessons.rationale: The self-controlled act in the world free of attraction and repulsion, ending all pain.
lessons.old_text: Move among temptations without craving or aversion to find peace.

verses.text: But the self-controlled man, moving among objects with the senses restrained and free from attraction and repulsion, attains peace.
In that peace, all pains are destroyed; for the intellect of the tranquil-minded soon becomes steady.

lessons.text: No peace or joy without a steady mind.
lessons.rationale: The negative chain: no wisdom, meditation, peace, or happiness for the unsteady.
lessons.old_text: Without a steady mind there is no peace or joy.

verses.text: There is no knowledge of the Self for the unsteady, and no meditation is possible for the unsteady, and no peace for the unmeditative, and how can there be happiness for one who has no peace?

lessons.text: One stray impulse drags your focus off course.
lessons.rationale: The wind-and-boat image: one loose sense drags away discrimination, so restrain them all.
lessons.old_text: Rein in the wandering senses that pull the mind off course.

verses.text: For the mind, which follows in the wake of the wandering senses, carries away his discrimination, as the wind carries away a boat on the waters.
Therefore, O mighty-armed Arjuna, his knowledge is steady whose senses are completely restrained from sense objects.

lessons.text: What everyone chases, the wise often question.
lessons.rationale: The night-and-day inversion of the sage's values against the world's.
lessons.old_text: What the world chases, the wise quietly turn from.

verses.text: That which is night to all beings, in that the self-controlled man is awake; when all beings are awake, that is night for the sage who sees.

lessons.text: Let desires come and go like rivers to the sea.
lessons.rationale: The ocean image: peace comes to one whom desires enter without disturbing.
lessons.old_text: Let desires enter and pass like rivers into the sea.

verses.text: He attains peace into whom all desires enter, just as waters enter the ocean which, filled from all sides, remains unmoved; but not the man who is full of desires.

lessons.text: Let go of craving, ownership, and ego.
lessons.rationale: Peace through abandoning longing, the sense of 'mine,' and egoism.
lessons.old_text: Drop craving, ownership, and ego to arrive at peace.

verses.text: That person attains peace who, abandoning all desires, moves about without longing, without the sense of ownership, and without egoism.

lessons.text: True peace holds steady even at the end.
lessons.rationale: The concluding Brahmic state, undeluded and attained even at life's end.
lessons.old_text: Reach this state and even death cannot delude you.

verses.text: O son of Pritha, this is the eternal state, the Brahmic seat. Attaining this, one is not deluded. Being established in it, one attains oneness with Brahman even at the end of life.
